title: Selyodka pod shuboy
subtitle: Russian herring under a fur coat
excerpt: Pickled herring layered with cooked potato, carrot, and beetroot, each layer masked with mayonnaise and chilled until firm. Russia's most theatrical salad, dramatic in colour and completely unlike anything else.
sourceNotes: Selyodka pod shuboy (herring under a fur coat) was invented in the early twentieth century, with various origin stories placing it at restaurants, taverns, or workers' canteens during the revolutionary period. The dish became a fixture of Soviet New Year celebrations, served alongside Olivier salad as the two essential festive dishes. The name refers to the layers of vegetables covering the herring as if dressing it in a fur coat. The beetroot on top gives the finished salad its vivid magenta surface.

content.text: Selyodka pod shuboy is assembled in a deep dish or bowl, layer by layer, each component cooled and grated before going in. The order is fixed: herring at the base, then onion, then potato, then carrot, then beetroot on top. Each layer is masked with mayonnaise. The finished salad is covered and chilled for at least three hours (overnight is better) until the mayonnaise has set the layers together into something that can be sliced cleanly and holds its shape on the plate.

All vegetables should be cooked, cooled, and grated rather than diced, grating creates a denser, more uniform texture that holds better in layers. The herring at the base is finely chopped. Every layer is thin: no more than 1 cm per component.

items.name: Potato
items.prepNote: boiled whole, cooled, peeled, and coarsely grated

items.name: Carrot
items.prepNote: boiled whole, cooled, peeled, and coarsely grated

content.text: Ensure all vegetables are completely cold. In a deep serving dish or bowl, spread the {{herring}} in an even layer across the base. Cover with the {{red-onion}}. Spread a thin layer of {{mayonnaise}} over the onion. Add the grated {{potato}} in an even layer, then another spread of mayonnaise. Add the grated {{carrot}}, then mayonnaise. Add the grated {{eggs}}, then mayonnaise. Finally, spread the grated {{beetroot}} across the top in a thick, even layer, and mask completely with the remaining mayonnaise. The surface should be entirely pink.

Cover the dish tightly with cling film and refrigerate for a minimum of 3 hours. Overnight is better. The salad is served cold, scooped from the dish or sliced if made in a loaf tin for a more formal presentation. It does not need any additional seasoning: the herring and mayonnaise provide sufficient salt and fat.

items.fix: Chill for a minimum of 3 hours. Each mayonnaise layer should be generous enough to seal the layer below. It is the glue that holds the salad together
items.cause: Not chilled long enough, or mayonnaise layers too thin

items.fix: The beetroot should be completely cold before use and coarsely grated. Some colour bleeding is expected, but keeping the beetroot cold and on top minimises it
items.cause: Beetroot too warm when added, or too finely grated
items.symptom: Beetroot bleeding red into lower layers

items.fix: Use lighter, thinner layers of mayonnaise between components. The salad should be cohesive, not swimming in dressing
items.symptom: Salad tastes too rich and heavy

content.text: Selyodka pod shuboy and Olivier salad are the two dishes without which a Russian New Year celebration cannot properly begin. They are made in advance, require no cooking on the day, and can be prepared in quantity for large groups. The fur coat salad is the more visually striking of the two, its surface of deep magenta beetroot and mayonnaise is unmistakable. The herring underneath, invisible until the first spoonful breaks the surface, is the discovery. It is a theatrical dish, which is fitting for a celebration.
